coredata.dc:title: Lanthanide ternary complex as a fluorescent probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of copper ions based on selective recognition and photoinduced electron transfer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jiejie
Pei, Jianchuan
Li, Gaiyan
coredata.dc:description: Copper ions have a very important role in human health, industrial and agricultural production. Herein, lanthanide ternary complex of 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA)-Eu3+-polyethyleneimine (PEI) as a fluorescent probe was thus fabricated for highly sensitive and selective detection of copper ions. PEI itself is non-fluorescent, the PEI-Eu3+complex is also non-fluorescent, and PEI has specific recognition to copper ions due to its higher affinity ability to copper ion than other metal ions. It was found that Cu2+ ions cannot quench the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ in the DPA-Eu3+ system, while in the DPA-Eu3+-PEI system, Cu2+ ions can greatly quench the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ due to photoinduced electron transfer (PET). The luminescent and quenching mechanism was also discussed in detail. The DPA-Eu3+-PEI probe not only has high sensitivity and selectivity, but also has very rapid fluorescence response and the response time is only 1 min. A good linear relationship between the fluorescence ratios of F0/F and the concentrations of Cu2+ was obtained in the range of 0.02 ∼ 10.0 μM (R2 = 0.998), and the limit of detection (LOD) is 8.0 nM. The probe was successfully applied for the detection of Cu2+ ions in the lake and river water samples, wastewater and urine samples. This work may provide a new strategy for fabricating simple and effective fluorescence probe and a promising application for the rapid and on-site detection in environmental monitoring and biological fluids.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Europium (III) ions
Ternary complex
Copper ions
Fluorescent probe
Photoinduced electron transfer

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Strategy and mechanism for the Cu2+ detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China ( LZ20B070001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra (a) and UV–vis absorption spectra (b)、(c)、(d) of different systems. Conditions: DPA: 400.0 μM, Eu3+: 50.0 μM, PEI: 25.0 μM, Cu2+: 1.0 μM, pH = 7.0.
Fluorescence lifetime of the DPA-Eu3+-PEI system in the absence and presence of Cu2+. Conditions: DPA: 400.0 μM, Eu3+: 50.0 μM, PEI: 25.0 μM, λem = 616 nm, pH = 7.0.

Effects of the composition ratios (molar ratio) of [DPA]/[Eu3+] (a), [PEI]/[Eu3+] (b), and pH (c) on the quenching efficiency of the DPA-Eu3+-PEI system caused by Cu2+ ions, (d) Effect of the equilibration time on the fluorescence intensity of the DPA-Eu3+-PEI system in the presence of Cu2+ ions. F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the DPA-Eu3+-PEI system in the absence and presence of Cu2+ ions, respectively. Conditions: DPA: 400.0 μM, Eu3+: 50.0 μM, PEI: 25.0 μM, Cu2+: 1.0 μM, pH = 7.0.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the DAP-Eu3+-PEI system as a function of different concentrations of Cu2+; (b) Linear relationship between the fluorescence ratios of F0/F and the concentrations of Cu2+. Conditions: DPA: 400.0 μM, Eu3+: 50.0 μM, PEI: 25.0 μM, pH = 7.0.

Selectivity of the DPA-Eu3+-PEI probe in the presence of Cu2+ ions and various metal ions (a) or anions (b). Conditions: Cu2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cr3+: 1.0 μM, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Zn2+: 100.0 μM, Fe3+, Ag+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Hg2+ and anions: 10.0 μM, DPA: 400.0 μM, Eu3+: 50.0 μM, PEI: 25.0 μM, pH = 7.0.
Schematic illustration of the DPA-Eu3+-PEI fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu2+ ions.
Comparison of the proposed method with other reported analytical approaches in the analytical performance of Cu2+ ions.
Recoveries of Cu2+ ions in the spiked lake and river water samples determined by the proposed method.
Determination of Cu2+ ions in the actual wastewater and urine samples.

head.ce:title: Lanthanide ternary complex as a fluorescent probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of copper ions based on selective recognition and photoinduced electron transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment and Resources, Zhejiang A&F University, Hangzhou 311300, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environment and Resources, Zhejiang A&F University, Hangzhou 311300, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jinshan Environmental Monitoring Center, Shanghai 200540, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jinshan Environmental Monitoring Center, Shanghai 200540, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lanthanide ternary complex of 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA)-Eu3+-polyethyleneimine (PEI) as a fluorescent probe was fabricated for highly sensitive and selective detection of copper ions based on selective recognition and photoinduced electron transfer.

Copper ions have a very important role in human health, industrial and agricultural production. Herein, lanthanide ternary complex of 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA)-Eu3+-polyethyleneimine (PEI) as a fluorescent probe was thus fabricated for highly sensitive and selective detection of copper ions. PEI itself is non-fluorescent, the PEI-Eu3+complex is also non-fluorescent, and PEI has specific recognition to copper ions due to its higher affinity ability to copper ion than other metal ions. It was found that Cu2+ ions cannot quench the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ in the DPA-Eu3+ system, while in the DPA-Eu3+-PEI system, Cu2+ ions can greatly quench the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ due to photoinduced electron transfer (PET). The luminescent and quenching mechanism was also discussed in detail. The DPA-Eu3+-PEI probe not only has high sensitivity and selectivity, but also has very rapid fluorescence response and the response time is only 1 min. A good linear relationship between the fluorescence ratios of F0/F and the concentrations of Cu2+ was obtained in the range of 0.02 ∼ 10.0 μM (R2 = 0.998), and the limit of detection (LOD) is 8.0 nM. The probe was successfully applied for the detection of Cu2+ ions in the lake and river water samples, wastewater and urine samples. This work may provide a new strategy for fabricating simple and effective fluorescence probe and a promising application for the rapid and on-site detection in environmental monitoring and biological fluids.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper ion is a kind of trace element, which widely exists in nature and plays an important role in the biological processes, it’s the third most important metal ion in the human body after iron and zinc [1]. Copper ions have greater affinity with the thiol groups of some enzymes in human tissues, so they are often the key cofactor or catalytic center of these metalloproteinases [2]. Copper ions are also essential for bone formation and connective tissue growth. In a word, copper ions have a very important role in human health, industrial and agricultural production. However, excessive use of copper-containing reagents in agriculture or copper-containing industrial waste into environment will seriously contaminate soil and water resources. Excessive copper ions in the water body will also affect lower organisms which are more sensitive to it, for example, fish and algae, and in turn will significantly affect the self-purification ability of the water body [3]. Additionally, excessive copper ions in the environment can get into animals, plants, and microorganisms and finally enter the human body through bioconcentration. Excessive copper ions in the body can stimulate the digestive system and damage the function of liver and kidney [4], and also inhibit the activity of various enzymes, which causes a series of mental diseases such as Alzheimer’s disease, Wilson’s disease, Menk’s syndrome and many others [5]. Therefore, it’s of great significance to develop a simple, rapid and low-cost analytical approach for sensitive and selective detection of copper ions.

At present, various analytical techniques have been developed for detecting copper ions such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [6], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [7], electroanalysis [8], ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry (UV–vis) [9], and fluorimetry [10]. However, some of them requires expensive instruments, complicated sample pretreatment, high costs or time-consuming. For example, ICP-MS requires expensive instruments and high costs, electroanalysis is relatively unstable and susceptible to complicated matrix interference such as environmental and biological samples [11]. By contrast, fluorescence methods have attracted more attention due to easy operation, rapid response, high sensitivity and low cost. Nowadays, fluorescence probes for detecting copper ions mainly include carbon quantum dots [10,12], metal quantum dots [13,14], metal nanoclusters [15–17], MOFs [18–20] and composite probes [21–23]. Nevertheless, the preparation of these materials requires complicated synthesis steps, high temperature condition and relatively time-consuming, and most of them cannot be purchased or obtained from commodity markets, which will restrict their wide application. Therefore, developing a fluorescence probe with simplicity, easy preparation and operation for detecting copper ions is still necessary and significant. Recently, lanthanide-based fluorescence probes have exhibited great promise because of its excellent advantages such as long lifetimes, large Stokes shift, and narrow emission peaks, which has stimulated great development in the fields of optics, catalysis and sensors [24]. Generally, lanthanide ions such as Tb3+ and Eu3+ can emit its characteristic fluorescence in aqueous solution only when the antenna effect between ligands and lanthanide ions occurs [25]. So far, seldom fluorescent probes based on lanthanide complex for detecting copper ions have been reported [26].

In the present work, an interesting insight was found that Cu2+ ions cannot quench the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ in the 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA)-Eu3+ system, while in the DPA-Eu3+- polyethyleneimine (PEI) system, Cu2+ ions can greatly quench the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+. Moreover, PEI itself is non-fluorescent, the PEI-Eu3+complex is also non-fluorescent, and PEI has specific recognition to copper ions due to its higher affinity ability to copper ion than other metal ions. Lanthanide ternary complex of DPA-Eu3+-PEI as a fluorescent probe was thus fabricated for highly sensitive and selective detection of copper ions. The schematic illustration of the DPA-Eu3+-PEI fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu2+ ions was shown in Scheme 1 . The luminescent and quenching mechanism was also discussed in detail. The DPA-Eu3+-PEI probe not only has high selectivity and sensitivity with a LOD of 8.0 nM, but also has very rapid fluorescence response and the response time is only 1 min. The probe was successfully applied for the detection of Cu2+ ions in the lake and river water samples, wastewater and urine samples. This work may provide a new strategy for fabricating simple and effective fluorescence probe and a promising application for the rapid and on-site detection in environmental monitoring and biological fluids.

All chemicals or reagents are of at least analytical grade and used directly. 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) was purchased from J&K Technology (Beijing) Co. ltd., europium chloride hexahydrate (EuCl3·6H2O) and polyethyleneimine (PEI, M = 1800) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co. ltd., tris-(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane (Tris) was purchased from American AMRESCO company, copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O) and other inorganic salts were purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co. ltd. All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water. Ultrapure water used in the experiments was produced by a UPK/UPT ultrapure water system.

All fluorescence measurements were performed on a F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a UH5300 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed on an AVATAR 370 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet, America). FLSP920 full-function steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer was used to measure the fluorescence lifetimes (Edinburgh, England). An Agilent 7800 inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) was used to compare the determined results with the proposed method. All pH measurements were conducted on an MP 220 pH meter (Mettler Toledo, Switzerland).

The lake and river water samples were collected locally (Hangzhou, China), urine samples were collected from two volunteers, and wastewater samples were collected from a nearby Maternal and Child Health Hospital (Hangzhou, China). The lake and river water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane before determination. Urine and wastewater samples were digested with nitric acid, and then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane for determination.

A general procedure as the following: 500 μL of 100.0 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 7.0), 400 μL of 5.0 mM DPA, 250 μL of 1.0 mM Eu3+ and 125 μL of 1.0 mM PEI were successively added to a 5.0 mL test tube, then a certain concentration of Cu2+ (0.02 ∼ 10.0 μM) or the pretreated sample was added, the mixture solution was finally diluted to 5.0 mL using ultrapure water, mixed uniformly and allowed to incubate 5 min at the room temperature. The fluorescence intensities were measured at the excitation wavelength of 272 nm and emission wavelength of 616 nm, and the slit widths for excitation and emission were both fixed at 10 nm. For the selectivity test, possible coexisting metal ions or anions were added to replace Cu2+, and the same procedure as above was performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Strategy and mechanism for the Cu2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 1
a, at the excitation wavelength of 272 nm, the DPA-Eu3+ complex can emit the red characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ at 594 nm, 616 nm, and 695 nm, which are assigned to the 5D0−7F J (J = 1, 2, and 4) transitions [27]. This is owing to the coordination between carboxyl groups of DPA and Eu3+, which results in the energy transfer from DPA to Eu3+ via antenna effect (AE) [27]. The coordination status between DPA or PEI and Eu3+ and its luminescent mechanism were shown in Scheme 1. It can be manifested by UV–vis absorption spectra and FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. 1 b, DPA has an absorption peak at 271 nm, which mainly involves the absorption of pyridine ring and carbonyl functional groups. Upon the addition of Eu3+, a new absorption peak appears at 279 nm, this is because the carboxyl groups of DPA coordinate with Eu3+ and form a larger conjugated ring [28,29]. Also, it can be seen from Fig. S1 a that in the FT-IR spectra of DPA + Eu3+, the peaks at 1627 cm−1 and 1439 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of the CN and CC double bonds in the DPA pyridine ring, respectively [27]. The stretching vibration of OCO at 1727 cm−1 of DPA is shifted to 1750 cm−1, and virtually cannot be observed in DPA + Eu3+, this is because O-atoms in DPA can coordinate with Eu3+ ions [30]. PEI itself is non-fluorescent, and it can coordinate with Eu3+ to form the non-fluorescent PEI-Eu3+complex. It can be seen from Fig. S1 b that in the FT-IR curve of PEI, the characteristic peaks bands at 1570 cm−1 and 1462 cm−1 are attributed to asymmetric and symmetric bending vibration of primary amines (−NH2), while in the FT-IR spectra of PEI-Eu3+, both of them are shifted to 1543 cm−1 and 1446 cm−1, respectively. Meanwhile, the stretching vibration of primary amines shifts from 3474 to 3419 cm−1, which is due to the coordination between PEI and Eu3+ [31]. When PEI is added to the DPA-Eu3+ system, because of the coordination between PEI and Eu3+ [32,33], the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ has an obvious quenching compared with the DPA-Eu3+ system. The reason why PEI can quench the fluorescence of Eu3+ is that PEI has a large amount of primary ammonium ions, the coordination of ammonium ions with Eu3+ will reduce the stability of the triplet state in the coordination domain and thereby reduces the fluorescence. There is an interesting finding that Cu2+ ions cannot quench the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ in the DPA-Eu3+ system, while in the DPA-Eu3+-PEI system, Cu2+ ions can greatly quench the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+. When the concentration of Cu2+ ions is 1.0 μM, the quenching efficiency can attain 71 %. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching caused by Cu2+ ion has a good selectivity. The DPA-Eu3+-PEI complex as a fluorescence probe was thus fabricated for highly sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ ions.

As shown in Fig. 1b and 1d, upon the addition of Cu2+, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the DPA-Eu3+ system do not have any change, the same situation also occurs in the DPA-Eu3+-PEI system, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching caused by Cu2+ ions is not a static quenching because the static quenching is usually based on the coordination interaction and causes the change of UV–vis absorption spectra [34]. Generally, fluorescence quenching is caused by the dynamic quenching or static quenching or both [35,36]. The most significant feature of dynamic quenching is the change in the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophore [37], so the fluorescence lifetimes of the DPA-Eu3+-PEI system in the absence and presence of Cu2+ ions were measured. As shown in Fig. 2 , after the addition of Cu2+ ions, the fluorescence lifetime of the DPA-Eu3+-PEI system decreases from 1.61 ms to 0.96 and 0.45 ms, indicating that the fluorescence quenching is ascribed to the dynamic quenching. The dynamic quenching could be further manifested by the Stern-Volmer equation, F0/F = 1 + 2.654[Cu2+] (R2 = 0.998), the F0/F value has a good linear relationship with Cu2+ ions. Dynamic quenching is usually caused by non-radiative charge transfer between the quencher and the excited molecules of the fluorescent substance. Due to many amino groups on the surface of PEI, these amino groups tend to interact with Cu2+ to form the DPA-Eu3+-PEI-Cu2+ exciplex, charge transfer can occur from the DPA-Eu3+ fluorophore to PEI-copper ions and results in the fluorescence quenching, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching caused by Cu2+ ions is derived from photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [38]. When the excited state molecules of fluorescent substance collide with quenching agent molecules, some exciplex may be formed, and PET process usually does not change the absorption spectrum of fluorescent substances. The absorption spectra of DPA-Eu3+-PEI do not have any change in the presence of Cu2+ ions (Fig. 1 d), and FT-IR spectra of the DPA-Eu3+-PEI (Fig. S1a) also cannot be changed by Cu2+ ions, which further manifests that the fluorescence quenching caused by Cu2+ ions is derived from PET [39].

In order to improve the sensitivity of the fluorescent probe, the experimental conditions such as the composition ratios (molar ratio) of [DPA]/[Eu3+] and [PEI]/[Eu3+], pH and equilibrium time were optimized. Effects of the composition ratios of [DPA]/[Eu3+] and [PEI]/[Eu3+] on the fluorescence intensities of the DPA-Eu3+-PEI system caused by Cu2+ ions were shown in Figure S2 . When the composition ratio of [PEI]/[Eu3+] of 1:2 was fixed, effect of the composition ratios of [DPA]/[Eu3+] on the quenching efficiency was shown in Fig. 3
a. As can be seen from Fig. 3 a, when the composition ratio of [DPA]/[Eu3+] is less than 8:1, the quenching efficiency caused by Cu2+ gradually increases, when the composition ratio [DPA]/[Eu3+] is more than 8:1, the quenching efficiency attains equilibrium. The composition ratio [DPA]/[Eu3+] of 8:1 was thus selected for further experiments.

When the composition ratio [DPA]/[Eu3+] of 8:1 was fixed, namely, 50.0 μM of Eu3+ and 400 μM of DPA were used, effect of the composition ratios of [PEI]/[Eu3+] on the quenching efficiency was shown in Fig. 3 b. When the composition ratio of [PEI]/[Eu3+] is 0.5:1, the quenching efficiency attains the maximum. When the composition ratios of [PEI]/[Eu3+] continue to increase, the quenching efficiency shows a downward trend, indicating that excessive PEI will reduce the quenching ability of Cu2+. This is because when the content of PEI is excessive, there exists free PEI in the solution, these free PEI will compete with the DPA-Eu3+-PEI complex to bind Cu2+ ions, which makes part of Cu2+ ions unable to interact with the fluorophore of the DPA-Eu3+-PEI complex and results in reducing the quenching efficiency. The composition ratio of [PEI]/[Eu3+] of 0.5:1 was chosen for further experiments.

pH may have a great effect on the fluorescence intensity of the system especially fluorescent substances with some acidic or alkaline groups such as carboxyl and ammonium groups. As shown in Fig. 3 c, pH has a great influence on the quenching efficiency of the DPA-Eu3+-PEI system caused by Cu2+ ions. With the increase of pH, the quenching efficiency increases continuously, attains the maximum value at pH = 9.0, and then gradually decreases when pH further increases. Considering the precipitation effect of Cu2+ and Eu3+ ions in the alkaline environment, pH of 7.0 was chosen for further experiments. Equilibrium time also has some effect on the fluorescence intensity of the system. As shown in Fig. 3 d, Cu2+ ions have a very quick fluorescence response, the fluorescence quenching caused by Cu2+ can be completed within 1 min and remains stable at least 30 min. Equilibrium time of 5 min is chosen for the whole experiments.

The analytical performance of the DPA-Eu3+-PEI probe was investigated under the optimal experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 4
a, the fluorescence intensity at 616 nm is gradually quenched with the increase of the Cu2+ concentration (0 ∼ 10.0 μM). Fig. 4 b shows a good linear relationship between the fluorescence ratios of F0/F and the concentrations of Cu2+ in the range of 0.02 ∼ 10.0 μM, the regression equation is F0/F = 2.654 [Cu2+] + 1 (R2 = 0.998), and the limit of detection (LOD, 3σ/K, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution and K is the slope of the calibration curve) is 8.0 nM, which is far lower than the maximum limit of Cu2+ concentration in drinking water (20 μM) regulated by U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). Obviously, the sensitivity of the proposed method can meet the demand of environmental monitoring. As shown in Table 1 , the detection limit of the fluorescent probe is comparable or lower than most the previously reported fluorescent or other methods.

Good selectivity for a fluorescent probe is quite important. The selectivity of the DPA-Eu3+-PEI probe for the Cu2+ detection was evaluated in the presence of possible coexisting interferential substances including common metal ions such as Na+, K+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, and anions such as F-, Cl-, Br-, I-, NO3 –, CH3COO–, CO3 2–, SO4 2-, SO3 2-, PO4 3-, S2-, SCN-. As shown in Fig. 5 a and 5b, among these possible coexisting interferential substances, except Co2+ and Ni2+ have a slight interference, other species almost do not interfere with the determination of Cu2+. Even some species their concentrations are 10 times or 100 times of Cu2+, they still do not interfere with the detection of Cu2+, suggesting the proposed probe has good selectivity for Cu2+ ions.

To check the practicability of the proposed method, some environmental water samples such as river and lake water samples, wastewater and biological fluids such as urine samples were collected and only pretreated by a simple treatment, then measured by the proposed method. The results of the spiked lake and river water samples determined by the proposed method were shown in Table 2 . All the recoveries in the spiked lake and river water samples are in the range of 96.9 %∼103.7 % and RSDs are less than 2.4 %. As shown in Table 3 , the concentrations of Cu2+ in wastewater and urine samples determined by the proposed method are in good agreement with those measured by ICP-MS, and the agreement rates are in the range of 84.9 ∼ 98.6 %, indicating that the fluorescent probe is an effective tool for the detection of Cu2+ in various environmental water samples and biological fluid samples.

In summary, lanthanide ternary complex of DPA-Eu3+-PEI as a fluorescent probe was fabricated for highly sensitive and selective detection of copper ions based on the selective recognition to Cu2+ ions and fluorescence quenching via photoinduced electron transfer (PET). The DPA-Eu3+-PEI probe not only has high sensitivity and selectivity, but also has very rapid fluorescence response and the response time is only 1 min. Moreover, the fabrication of the probe is quite simple and does not need the complicated material preparation, all the chemicals or reagents can purchase from commodity markets, and can accomplish the rapid and on-site detection. Surface waters such as lake and river water samples only need a brief filter, while for wastewater and biological fluid samples, digesting using nitric acid is necessary to eliminate the interference from complicated organic compounds. This work will provide a promising application for the rapid and on-site detection in environmental monitoring and biological fluids.

Jiejie Wang: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Jianchuan Pei: Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Gaiyan Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LZ20B070001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122287.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
